prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computational Chemistry / Structure-Based Drug Design who understands how scientific ideas evolve.
Below is an important research lineage in this area, where each paper evolves from its predecessors:

**[2017] Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry**
Key Contribution: MPNN: unified message passing framework for molecular property prediction; state-of-the-art on QM9 quantum chemical properties.
Target Problem: No unified framework for applying deep learning to molecular graphs; diverse architectures prevent systematic comparison.
Core Method: Abstract message passing: node features updated by aggregating transformed neighbor messages; readout function pools atom features to molecular properties.
Observed Evidence: MPNN achieves state-of-the-art on 11 of 13 QM9 targets; unifies ConvMol, Weave, and other graph NNs under single framework.
Remaining Limitation: Predicts molecular properties from 2D graphs; cannot model 3D structure; no binding affinity prediction; no protein-ligand interaction.
Expected Result/Claim: MPNN: unified message passing framework for molecular property prediction; state-of-the-art on QM9 quantum chemical properties.

  ↓

**[2017] Atomic Convolutional Networks for Predicting Protein-Ligand Binding Affinity**
Key Contribution: AtomConv: applies MPNN-style graph convolutions to 3D protein-ligand complexes; predicts binding affinity from co-crystal structures.
Target Problem: MPNN predicts isolated molecular properties; drug discovery requires protein-ligand binding affinity from 3D complex structure.
Core Method: Atomic convolution over protein-ligand complex: atom features updated by distance-weighted 3D neighbor messages; separate encoders for protein and ligand atoms.
Observed Evidence: AtomConv matches or exceeds Random Forest baselines on PDBbind binding affinity prediction; demonstrates DL applicability to structure-based design.
Remaining Limitation: Requires co-crystal structure (rarely available for new targets); cannot predict docked pose; limited to small molecule ligands.
Expected Result/Claim: AtomConv: applies MPNN-style graph convolutions to 3D protein-ligand complexes; predicts binding affinity from co-crystal structures.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry)
  Change Summary: MPNN's molecular graph convolution framework radiates from isolated molecular property prediction to protein-ligand complex binding affinity prediction in 3D.
  Main Changed Field: Target Problem
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work changed; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: reuse in a shifted target problem driven by target problem; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Target Problem | source=No unified framework for applying deep learning to molecular graphs; diverse architectures prevent systematic comparison. | new=MPNN predicts isolated molecular properties; drug discovery requires protein-ligand binding affinity from 3D complex structure.

  ↓

**[2022] EquiBind: Geometric Deep Learning for Drug Binding Structure Prediction**
Key Contribution: EquiBind: predicts protein-ligand binding pose directly without exhaustive docking search; SE(3)-equivariant GNN for both protein and ligand.
Target Problem: Classical docking (AutoDock) requires exhaustive conformational search; AtomConv cannot predict binding pose, only score given poses.
Core Method: SE(3)-equivariant GNN processes protein and ligand separately; cross-attention predicts binding geometry; rigid-body alignment to protein pocket.
Observed Evidence: 1200× faster than traditional docking; competitive RMSD on PDBbind test set; enables screening large compound libraries.
Remaining Limitation: Ligand flexibility poorly handled (rigid docking assumption); lower accuracy than physics-based methods on difficult targets; no uncertainty quantification.
Expected Result/Claim: EquiBind: predicts protein-ligand binding pose directly without exhaustive docking search; SE(3)-equivariant GNN for both protein and ligand.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Atomic Convolutional Networks for Predicting Protein-...)
  Change Summary: EquiBind extends AtomConv from scoring given poses to directly predicting binding geometry; same SE(3)-equivariant GNN framework, adds cross-attention for pose prediction.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source changed; new work changed
  Field Change Summary: local change from the source driven by method; changed method, target problem, claim; not reused observation, limitation; added method, target problem, observation, limitation.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Atomic convolution over protein-ligand complex: atom features updated by distance-weighted 3D neighbor messages; separate encoders for protein and ligand atoms. | new=SE(3)-equivariant GNN processes protein and ligand separately; cross-attention predicts binding geometry; rigid-body alignment to protein pocket.

  ↓

**[2023] DiffDock: Diffusion Steps, Twists, and Turns for Molecular Docking**
Key Contribution: DiffDock: diffusion over SE(3) ligand pose (translation, rotation, torsion angles); generates diverse binding pose hypotheses; surpasses all prior blind docking methods.
Target Problem: EquiBind produces single rigid-body pose; real binding is flexible; need multi-modal pose distribution over all degrees of freedom.
Core Method: Generative diffusion over 3D pose space: separate diffusion processes for translation (R³), rotation (SO(3)), and torsion angles; confidence ranking of generated poses.
Observed Evidence: DiffDock achieves 38% top-1 success rate on PDBbind vs 23% for Glide (commercial); generates diverse pose hypotheses; state-of-the-art blind docking.
Remaining Limitation: Slow sampling (multiple denoising steps); still requires known protein structure; cannot generate novel ligands; no co-design of protein and ligand.
Expected Result/Claim: DiffDock: diffusion over SE(3) ligand pose (translation, rotation, torsion angles); generates diverse binding pose hypotheses; surpasses all prior blind docking methods.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: EquiBind: Geometric Deep Learning for Drug Binding St...)
  Change Summary: DiffDock replaces EquiBind's direct regression of single pose with diffusion over the full SE(3) pose distribution; generates diverse multi-modal hypotheses — fundamentally new generative paradigm.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source changed; new work changed
  Field Change Summary: main-method replacement driven by method; changed method, target problem, claim; not reused observation, limitation; added method, target problem, observation, limitation.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=SE(3)-equivariant GNN processes protein and ligand separately; cross-attention predicts binding geometry; rigid-body alignment to protein pocket. | new=Generative diffusion over 3D pose space: separate diffusion processes for translation (R³), rotation (SO(3)), and torsion angles; confidence ranking of generated poses.

  ↓

**[2024] Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3**
Key Contribution: AlphaFold3: unified diffusion-based model for structure prediction of proteins, DNA, RNA, small molecules, and their complexes; covers all biomolecular interaction types.
Target Problem: DiffDock requires separate protein structure prediction (AlphaFold2) and ligand docking; no unified model for all biomolecular complex types.
Core Method: Diffusion over 3D atomic coordinates for all molecule types; Pairformer trunk encodes inter-chain relationships; trained on PDB structures of all complex types.
Observed Evidence: AlphaFold3 improves by 50% over best prior methods on protein-ligand binding structure prediction; covers DNA/RNA/small molecule complexes.
Remaining Limitation: Not freely available for commercial drug discovery; does not model binding kinetics (only thermodynamic structures); cannot generate novel ligands.
Expected Result/Claim: AlphaFold3: unified diffusion-based model for structure prediction of proteins, DNA, RNA, small molecules, and their complexes; covers all biomolecular interaction types.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: DiffDock: Diffusion Steps, Twists, and Turns for Mole...)
  Change Summary: AlphaFold3 hybridizes DiffDock's diffusion-over-coordinates with AlphaFold2's Evoformer structure prediction into a unified model covering all biomolecular complex types.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source changed; new work changed; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: combining multiple source ideas driven by method; changed method, target problem, limitation; not reused observation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Generative diffusion over 3D pose space: separate diffusion processes for translation (R³), rotation (SO(3)), and torsion angles; confidence ranking of generated poses. | new=Diffusion over 3D atomic coordinates for all molecule types; Pairformer trunk encodes inter-chain relationships; trained on PDB structures of all complex types.

  ↓

**[2026] Interaction-constrained 3D Molecular Generation Using a Diffusion Model Enables Structure-Based Pharmacophore Modeling**
Key Contribution: DiffPharma: conditional diffusion model with interaction-specific neural networks generates drug candidates satisfying pharmacophore constraints derived from protein-ligand structures; moves beyond AlphaFold3 structure prediction to structure-conditioned molecular generation.
Target Problem: AlphaFold3 predicts binding structures but cannot generate novel ligands; DiffDock generates binding poses but not molecular structures; no model combines pharmacophore-constrained generation with 3D structural awareness.
Core Method: Conditional diffusion with interaction-specific neural networks that enforce pharmacophore constraints from protein-ligand structures; generates drug candidates satisfying geometric and chemical interaction requirements derived from the binding pocket.
Observed Evidence: DiffPharma generates pharmacophore-satisfying 3D molecules conditioned on protein-ligand structural information; advances structure-based drug design beyond pose prediction to active molecular generation.
Remaining Limitation: Optimizes for binding geometry (pharmacophore satisfaction) without explicit ADMET property optimization; binding geometry does not guarantee in vivo efficacy; multi-objective drug design (affinity + ADMET + synthesizability) not addressed.
Expected Result/Claim: DiffPharma: conditional diffusion model with interaction-specific neural networks generates drug candidates satisfying pharmacophore constraints derived from protein-ligand structures; moves beyond AlphaFold3 structure prediction to structure-conditioned molecular generation.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interac...)
  Change Summary: DiffPharma radiates AlphaFold3's structure prediction framework to structure-conditioned molecular generation: leverages protein-ligand structural information not just for prediction but to actively generate pharmacophore-satisfying drug candidates.
  Main Changed Field: Target Problem
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source changed; new work changed; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: reuse in a shifted target problem driven by target problem; changed method, target problem, limitation; not reused observation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, claim.
  Main Change: Target Problem | source=DiffDock requires separate protein structure prediction (AlphaFold2) and ligand docking; no unified model for all biomolecular complex types. | new=AlphaFold3 predicts binding structures but cannot generate novel ligands; DiffDock generates binding poses but not molecular structures; no model combines pharmacophore-constrai...


The key unresolved question at the frontier of this lineage:
DiffPharma generates pharmacophore-constrained molecules but optimizes only for binding geometry without explicit ADMET property optimization. What mechanism would enable joint multi-objective drug design — simultaneously satisfying pharmacophore constraints, binding affinity, ADMET properties, and synthetic accessibility within a single diffusion framework?

Based on the lineage history, source-to-new-work changes, and the current frontier question, generate a new research idea as the next evolutionary node.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
